title: Navigating the Tax Crossroads: Unpacking the Income Tax Department's Stance on AY 2026-27 and the New Era of ITR Filings
content:
# Navigating the Tax Crossroads: Unpacking the Income Tax Department's Stance on AY 2026-27 and the New Era of ITR Filings

Alright, fellow tax professionals, let's cut through the noise, shall we? The air has been thick with questions and a touch of uncertainty ever since the Income Tax Act, 2025, stepped onto the field. Many of us have been wondering, "What does this mean for our immediate filings? Does the 'old wine' still apply to our 'new bottle' of income?" Well, the Income Tax Department has provided some much-needed clarity, particularly concerning Assessment Year (AY) 2026-27 and the road ahead for ITR forms. And believe me, this isn't just a renumbering exercise; it's a foundational shift we need to understand thoroughly.

### The Dawn of a New Act, But Not for AY 2026-27

The biggest change, of course, is the **Income Tax Act, 2025**, which came into effect from April 1, 2026, completely replacing the decades-old Income Tax Act, 1961. This new Act aims to simplify our tax laws, streamline compliance, and bring in a more taxpayer-centric approach. But here’s the crucial clarification, the *dadi-amma ka nuskha* if you will: while the new Act is now law, its provisions will **not** govern your filings for Assessment Year 2026-27.

That's right. For income earned during the Financial Year (FY) 2025-26 (i.e., from April 1, 2025, to March 31, 2026), which we assess in AY 2026-27, the **Income Tax Act, 1961**, along with its associated rules and provisions, remains fully applicable. The Income Tax Department has explicitly stated that their e-filing portal will facilitate compliance under both the old and new Acts concurrently during this transition phase.

### Goodbye 'Assessment Year,' Hello 'Tax Year' – But When?

Another significant structural change brought by the Income Tax Act, 2025, is the abolition of the concepts of 'Previous Year' and 'Assessment Year.' Instead, we now have a single, unified concept: the **'Tax Year'**. This change is designed to reduce confusion, as the 'Tax Year' for which income is earned will also be the year of assessment.

However, and this is important, this 'Tax Year' concept is applicable only from April 1, 2026, onwards. This means income earned during FY 2026-27 (i.e., from April 1, 2026, to March 31, 2027) will be referred to as Tax Year 2026-27, and its return will be filed in 2027.

So, for all practical purposes, when you sit down to file your Income Tax Return for AY 2026-27 this July or August, you'll still be thinking in terms of "Previous Year" (FY 2025-26) and "Assessment Year" (AY 2026-27) under the good old 1961 Act. No need to file two ITRs for the same income period; the separation is clean.

### Upcoming ITR Forms: Familiarity with a Twist

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has already notified the ITR forms (ITR-1 to ITR-7) for AY 2026-27. While the form numbers themselves are familiar, there are some notable updates and revised eligibility criteria that taxpayers must be aware of:

*   **Expanded ITR-1 Eligibility:** Good news for small equity investors! Resident individuals can now report Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG) under **Section 112A** in ITR-1, provided these gains don't exceed ₹1.25 lakh and there are no capital losses to be carried forward. Earlier, even small LTCG often pushed taxpayers to ITR-2. *Thoda relief toh mila hai!*
*   **Detailed Capital Gains Reporting:** For those dealing with capital gains, especially from assets sold after July 23, 2024 (when new capital gains tax provisions came into effect), the revised ITR-2 and ITR-3 demand a more granular break-up of transactions.
*   **F&O Disclosure for Traders:** Taxpayers filing ITR-3 will see additional reporting requirements for Futures & Options (F&O) transactions. Turnover and income from F&O must now be disclosed separately, rather than being clubbed under broader business income.
*   **No New Forms for AY 2026-27:** While the Income Tax Rules, 2026, under the new Act introduce entirely new form numbers (e.g., Form 16 becomes Form 130, Form 26AS becomes Form 168), these new forms are for the Tax Year 2026-27 onwards. For AY 2026-27, we use the existing series of ITR forms (1-7) as modified.

### TDS/TCS in Transition: What Deductors Need to Know

This transition also impacts TDS and TCS provisions. The Act governing TDS depends on when the "earlier of the event of credit or payment" occurs.
*   If the credit or payment happened on or before March 31, 2026, the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961, are applicable.
*   If it occurred on or after April 1, 2026, the Income Tax Act, 2025, governs the deduction.

The new Act has consolidated all TDS/TCS provisions into new sections (Sections 392, 393, and 394), and introduced 4-digit numeric payment codes that replace the old section numbers for quarterly returns for Tax Year 2026-27. Deductors need to update their systems to reflect these changes to avoid validation failures. Also, CBDT circulars on TDS and TCS are now legally binding on deductors from April 1, 2026.

### Scrutiny & Compliance: Deadlines to Remember

The CBDT has also issued guidelines for the compulsory selection of income tax returns for complete scrutiny for FY 2025-26 (which is AY 2026-27). Taxpayers should note the statutory deadline for issuing scrutiny notices under **Section 143(2)** for returns filed during FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27) is **June 30, 2026**. If no such notice is issued by this date, your return is generally considered free from detailed examination under this section.

Key deadlines for AY 2026-27:
*   **July 31, 2026:** Due date for ITR-1 and ITR-2 filers (salaried individuals, capital gains income).
*   **August 31, 2026:** Due date for ITR-3 and ITR-4 filers (non-audit business and professional cases). This is an extended date for non-audit business and professional cases.
*   **October 31, 2026:** Due date for tax audit cases.
*   **November 30, 2026:** Due date for businesses requiring transfer pricing reports.
*   **December 31, 2026:** Last date to file a belated return for AY 2026-27.
*   **March 31, 2027:** Last date to file a revised return for AY 2026-27. This is a significant extension from the previous December 31 deadline.

### Navigating the Dual Regime Choice

For AY 2026-27, the option to choose between the **Old Tax Regime** (with deductions and exemptions) and the **New Tax Regime** (with lower slab rates but fewer deductions) still applies under the Income Tax Act, 1961. The new tax regime became the default for individuals and HUFs from AY 2024-25, but eligible taxpayers can continue to opt out and choose the old regime. Tax slabs generally remain unchanged for FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27) under both regimes.

### Final Thoughts

This financial year marks a unique transition, a sort of 'bridge' between two legislative eras. While the Income Tax Act, 2025, has introduced a modern framework and the 'Tax Year' concept, we are still very much operating under the shadow of the 1961 Act for our AY 2026-27 filings.

The key takeaway? Don't get caught in the crossfire of dates and Acts. Focus on the income earned during FY 2025-26, and remember that for this period, it's the old Act rules that apply to your ITR forms. Use the familiar ITR forms (albeit with minor updates), adhere to the prescribed due dates, and ensure all your disclosures are accurate. *Thoda sa dhyan rakhna padega, bas!*

For the true impact of the Income Tax Act, 2025, and its new forms and numbering, we'll shift our focus to compliance for Tax Year 2026-27, which will be due for filing next year. Until then, for AY 2026-27, let's keep calm and comply with the familiar.

## Read the Official Document
* [Download the Official Notification/Circular here](https://www.incometax.gov.in/iec/foportal/help/faqs/faqs-interplay-and-transition)

*Published on: 26 June 2026*
